Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor

 Agenda
• Systems and Systems Thinking
• Systems Mapping
• Where to Intervene?




                                 3
 Systems
                                           System
   “an interconnected set of elements that is coherently organized in a way that achieves
something…A system must consist of three kinds of things: elements, interconnections, and a
                                        function or purpose     “

                                           (Meadows 2009)




               Image: Cambridge Institute for Sustainability Leadership (CISL)                5

 Systems Thinking
 What is Systems Thinking?

      Systems thinking is a perspective of
                                                Systems thinking is a holistic method
      seeing and understanding systems as
                                                for understanding positive and
      wholes rather than as collections of
                                                negative influences on a problem and
      parts. A whole is a web of
                                                identifying the “big levers” for creating
      interconnections that creates emerging
                                                change (Ramina et al 2021, p.7).
      patterns (Peter Senge)




                                                When one element of a system
      A strong systems approach reveals key
                                                changes, the other parts will be
      interactions, anticipates consequences,
                                                affected and, in the end, the stability of
      and guides strategic action.
                                                the whole system.




                                                                                             8
 What is Systems Thinking?
                                        Considering all aspects of a
                                        system's behavior within its
                                        broader environment




                                        By focusing on parts of a
                                        system, we fail to understand
                                        the whole.


    The Blind Men and the Elephant
                                                                        9
    https://www.theblindelephant.com/
   Lacking a Systems Thinking… the solution to one problem can
easily lead to a new and bigger problem later on or somewhere else
                            in the system
       Lacking a Systems Thinking… the solution to one problem can easily lead to a new and bigger problem
                                  later on or somewhere else in the system


• Case Study: Plastic Bag Ban Without Systems Thinking
• Policy Goal: Reduce litter via single-use plastic bag bans
• Outcome:
➢ Minor drop in plastic bag use
➢ Offset by rise in purchases of other plastic bags (e.g. bin liners)
➢ No significant reduction in litter
➢ Increased costs for low-income households
➢ Public frustration weakened support for environmental policies

• Systems Thinking approach would include:
➢ Understanding consumer behavior
➢ Evaluating lifecycle of alternative bags
➢ Assessing waste management capacity

• Complementary strategies:
➢ Promote truly reusable bags
➢ Public education campaigns
➢ Invest in biodegradable alternatives
➢ Improve recycling infrastructure

• Key Takeaway:
➢ Narrow bans can backfire.
➢ A systems approach anticipates ripple effects and enables sustainable, equitable solutions.
Based on Macintosh et al. (2020)

   More readings:
   Plastic Bag Bans Have Lingering Impacts, Even After Repeals | School of Business
   In banning plastic bags we need to make sure we’re not creating new problems
   Plastic bag bans: Lessons from the Australian Capital Territory - ScienceDirect
 A problem in the system is everyone’s problem
 Key Benefits of Systems Thinking
                                   • Connects knowledge across domains; see relationships between seemingly unrelated
                                     concepts.

    Learning                       • Identify recurring patterns
                                   • Understand deeply the "why" behind phenomena.
                                   • Example: Medical students using systems thinking don't just see symptoms but understand
                                     how body systems interact to produce those symptoms.



                                   • Identifies leverage points


  Improving
                                   • Prevents unintended consequences; anticipate how changes might affect the whole system
                                   • Breaks persistent problems
                                   • Example: Tackling obesity by addressing food access, urban design, and socioeconomic
                                     factors.




                                   • Navigate complex transformations by identifying interdependencies.

 Transforming                      • Enable innovation, fosters sustainable solutions
                                   • Enable long-term change by redesigning systems.
                                   • Example: Cities go beyond recycling by integrating renewable energy and sustainable transport.




  Source: Stanford PACS Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society
                                                                                                                                 13
 Systems Thinking helps us to …
Shift the focus from events and behavioral patterns (which are symptoms of problems)
toward systemic structures and the underlying mental models that shape them.




                Source: Senge (1996)
 Systems Thinking helps us to …
 … find the most important places for intervention to change the
 long‐term behaviour of a system.




           Source: Senge (1996)                                    15
 Systems thinking is seeking to understand the
connections among elements in the system
                               ▪   What depends on what?
                               ▪   What is causing what?
                               ▪   Where are information flows?
                               ▪   Where control decisions are made?
                               ▪   What information flows are critical?
                               ▪   How to best engage stakeholders?
                               ▪   How best to manage or intervene in
                                   the system for desired results?


                                                                       16
   Example: Application of systems thinking – Urban mobility
                                                  Urban Mobility Challenges


                                   Current Focus: Electric Vehicles (EV) Adoption
                                          Advances technology but does not transform the system
                                            Requires infrastructure changes (charging stations)

                                                        Urban Spatial Planning
         Modal Shift                                                                               Enable Mobility Flexibility
                                                 Design cities with homes, workspaces,
 Encourage public transport,                                                                        Work from home, virtual
                                                    and leisure areas within walking
     walking, and cycling                                                                                  meetings
                                                                distance
Reduce reliance on private cars                                                                          E-commerce
                                                 Improve public transport accessibility


                                       Outcome: Smarter, More Sustainable Urban Mobility

  Source: Adapted from Dr Theo Hacking, Cambridge Institute for Sustainability Leadership (CISL)                                 17
 T hink systematically
If we don’t think
systematically when
planning for change,
sometimes we
misinterpret what’s
going on and what
needs to be ‘fixed’

                        18
 Systems Mapping
 Systems Mapping
Definition: Systems mapping is a visual representation of the
components of a system and their interactions. It helps in
understanding the complexity and interdependencies within a system.
Usage:
▪ Provides tools to see the whole picture.
▪ To identify key stakeholders, processes and outcomes.
▪ Navigate its complexities by considering context and interacting
   factors like economics, politics and global trends.
▪ Diagnose problems and identifying leverage points for
   interventions.
                                                                  20
 Systems Mapping

Systems mapping helps analyze how a system functions and identify intervention
opportunities, bridging the ‘Understand’ and ‘Design’ phases. It examines system
components and their relationships, using two main map types:


          Actor maps show people or organizations in a system, including
          stakeholder maps.


          Factor maps highlight causes and effects, such as policies, processes,
          behaviors and beliefs.



                                                                                   21
 Actor Maps
Stakeholder Maps
 Stakeholder Maps
                                                 Example
                                             ▪    Visually represent stakeholders and their relationships,
                                             ▪    Help in understanding and managing complex systems
                                                  by identifying key players and their potential influence
                                             Key Elements of a Stakeholder Map:
                                             ▪    Identifying Stakeholders: Determine who the key
                                                  stakeholders are, both internal and external.
                                             ▪    Categorization: Group stakeholders based on their
                                                  influence, interest and impact on the project or system.
                                             ▪    Prioritization: Identify which stakeholders require the
                                                  most attention and engagement.
                                             ▪    Relationships: Show how stakeholders interact with each
                                                  other and with the project/system.
                                             ▪    Visual Representation: Use diagrams, charts, or other
                                                  visual tools to map the stakeholders and their
                                                  relationships.




    (IDEO U, n.d., What is a Systems Map?)                                                                   23
 Stakeholder Maps
                                         Example
                               Key stakeholders and their relationships: five primary stakeholders (Teachers, Principals,
                               Students, Families, and the Local School Board) and shows how they relate to each other
                               through directional arrows.

                               Types of relationships: The map uses symbols to denote different types of interactions:

                                  ▪   Heart symbols ( ): caring relationships or emotional support (connecting
                                      Teachers→Students, Principals→Students, and Families→Students)

                                  ▪   Information symbols (i): knowledge or information flow (connecting
                                      Teachers→Principals and to Students)

                                  ▪   Dollar symbols ($): financial resources or budgetary influence (connecting Local
                                      School Board→Principals and to Teachers)

                               Directional influence: The arrows illustrate how influence flows through the system, showing
                               that some relationships are unidirectional while others involve mutual influence.

                               Systemic layers: suggests different layers of the educational system, from governance (School
                               Board) to administration (Principals) to implementation (Teachers) to the ultimate
                               beneficiaries (Students), with Families providing external support.

                               Feedback loops: between Teachers, Principals and Students, illustrating how actions in one
                               part of the system can cycle back through other components.




    (IDEO U, n.d., What is a Systems Map?)                                                                           24
   Factor Maps
    Cluster Diagrams
Multiple C ause D iagrams
 Mapping Tool 1: Cluster Diagrams

▪ Similar to a mind map
▪ Serves as a starting point for
  factor maps.
▪ Helps visualize key factors
  around an issue and their
  connections.
▪ Begins with the central topic,
  then brainstorm related
  causes, consequences, and
  influences.

        (New Philanthropy Capital (NPC), n.d.)
                                                 26
  Mapping Tool 2:                                                  Multiple cause diagrams

 ▪      Provide an overview of the connections and
        causes in a system.
 ▪      Are often used externally to share a
        summary of a systems analysis.

Example: a multi cause diagram the failure of a
‘get tough’ policy on sick leave at work

▪ Strict Policies → Reluctance to take sick leave
▪ Employees fear consequences, so they come to work sick (e.g flu).
▪ Infected employees → Flu spreads
▪ Sick employees infect colleagues, increasing flu cases.
▪ Increased workload & stress → Weaker immune systems
▪ More work and stress impair health, leading to longer recovery times
  and chronic illness.
▪ More sick employees → Productivity decrease
▪ As more workers fall ill, productivity declines -> the policy’s goal fails



                      (New Philanthropy Capital (NPC), n.d.)
                                                                                            27
 Systems Mapping: Pros & Cons
Benefits                                Limitations
▪   Understand system dynamics         ▪ Subjectivity: Maps reflect contributors’
                                         perspectives, making diverse participation
▪   Collaborative process                essential.
▪   Engaging communication tool
                                       ▪ Risk of oversimplification: Generalizing
▪   Strategic insights for action        experiences can overlook important nuances, such
                                         as differences in racial or socioeconomic impacts.
▪   Provides a comprehensive view
    of the system, promotes holistic   ▪ Complexity & expertise required: Effective mapping
    thinking.                            can be time-consuming and demands knowledge of
                                         systems thinking.



                                                                                      28
 Where to Intervene?
 The Spectrum of Interventions in a System
Focus on Symptoms: may address visible, urgent symptoms
but this narrow focus, can miss deeper, systemic issues.
Treating Symptoms vs. Root Causes: Solutions targeting
immediate causes may provide short-term relief but fail to
deliver lasting change if underlying problems remain
unaddressed.
Systems Change: Sustainable solutions require tackling root
causes, but this involves:
▪   Navigating complex, interconnected factors
▪   Changing entrenched behaviors
▪   Overcoming political resistance
▪   Investing significant time and effort


      Source: Stanford PACS Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society   30
 Example: The Spectrum of Interventions in a System:
Food Security Solutions at Different Levels

address the root causes of    consistent access to meals.    policies.
food insecurity.                  Limitation: Hunger             Solution: Sustainable, long-
                              persists without addressing    term reduction in food

                                                                                         31
    Systems Thinking &
Social Entrepreneurship
                   Global Challenges Demand
            Systems Thinking & Systemic Solutions




 Source: Budinich and Mones (2025)

Entrepreneurs and innovators must apply systems thinking to identify root causes rather than
symptoms of complex problems while recognizing the interconnectedness of issues
(Budinich & Mones 2025)
